summary.headline: Step 5b/5c closed most of the pass-1 backlog. The repo-hygiene gap (bp-3) is largely resolved (LICENSE-MIT + LICENSE-APACHE + SECURITY.md + dependabot.yml all landed), the clippy allow-list prune (bp-1) dropped from 95 to 51 entries, missing_docs is wired on 4/6 crates (bp-4 partial), the RedbSource(#[from] redb::Error) variant preserves source chains (bp-2 largely closed), coverage.yml + mutants.yml + bench.yml workflows exist (bp-6 + bp-11 closed), and ProptestConfig env (bp-5 partial — PROPTEST_CASES=1024, not the recommended 2048). One new pass-2 finding via the verify-don't-trust pass: the workspace has zero #[non_exhaustive] on any public error enum, which is 2026 thiserror-2 idiom for crates that will grow their variant set (compromise #3 extracted ErrorCode to benten-errors, which is the most obviously growing enum in the codebase). Three documentation-level partial closures (bp-2, bp-4, bp-12) reveal the verify-don't-trust pattern: Cargo.toml bp-1 prose says 'down from 95 entries to the genuine Phase-1-scope compromises' but the actual count is 51, not 46 as the commit message claimed; benten-eval/benten-engine's crate-root #![warn(missing_docs)] is immediately neutralized by an inner #![allow(missing_docs, reason = 'TODO(phase-2-docs)')] — the warn line reads as coverage but fires on nothing. Flagging these as bp-17 (non_exhaustive) and updating bp-1/bp-4 dispositions accordingly.

pass1_disposition_details.bp-1: Cargo.toml lint allow-list went from ~95 to 51 entries (counted 51 'allow' lines in [workspace.lints.clippy]). Commit message '95->46' is slightly off (51 actual); the R6 Wave 2 comment block at lines 46-48 is accurate in spirit. Kept: structural/namespacing (module_name_repetitions, doc_markdown), Phase-1 numeric (cast_* family), R3 red-phase residuals (allow_attributes, unused_self, unnecessary_wraps, items_after_statements, match_same_arms, etc.), and 2024-edition style relaxations (manual_let_else, single_match, needless_pass_by_value). Every remaining entry has a load-bearing 'reason =' prose. Net effect: pedantic gate is now a real gate rather than a cosmetic tag — pass-1 concern largely addressed. Residual 6 lints from the pass-1 non-negotiable deny-list (cast_possible_truncation, cast_sign_loss, shadow_unrelated, needless_pass_by_value, manual_let_else, doc_markdown) are still allowed with Phase-2 deferral reasons. Accept as partially-closed; keep on Phase-2 tighten list.
pass1_disposition_details.bp-2: GraphError gained RedbSource(#[from] redb::Error) at crates/benten-graph/src/lib.rs:74-75. The six redb sub-error From impls (lines 169-193) funnel through redb::Error so std::error::Error::source() walks the chain correctly. The legacy Redb(String) and Decode(String) variants are retained at lines 84/92 with clear prose saying they're test-fixture injection points + internal 'post-commit handle missing' bookkeeping. Production conversion sites use RedbSource. The 'Same treatment recommended for CoreError::Serialize(String)' bit of pass-1's suggested-fix is still open — CoreError::Serialize still carries a String payload per benten-core/src/lib.rs (unchanged). Accept as partially-closed; CoreError::Serialize remains as a drive-by Phase-2 item.
pass1_disposition_details.bp-3: Repo hygiene: LICENSE-MIT (19 lines, canonical MIT text) and LICENSE-APACHE (202 lines, canonical Apache-2.0 text) landed at repo root. SECURITY.md (37 lines) landed at repo root. .github/dependabot.yml (71 lines) covers cargo + npm (root, bindings/napi, packages/engine, tools/create-benten-app) + github-actions with weekly Monday schedule. STILL MISSING: CODE_OF_CONDUCT.md (not present), .github/ISSUE_TEMPLATE/ directory (not present — checked with ls, directory does not exist), .github/PULL_REQUEST_TEMPLATE.md (not present). The big-ticket items (LICENSE for crates.io, SECURITY for disclosure, dependabot for drift prevention) are closed. The community-contribution items (CoC, issue/PR templates) remain. Accept as partially-closed; the remaining items are low-cost and could be closed in a 30-minute drive-by.
pass1_disposition_details.bp-4: #![warn(missing_docs)] lines present on all 6 crates. BUT: crates/benten-eval/src/lib.rs:12-15 and crates/benten-engine/src/lib.rs:41-44 IMMEDIATELY re-allow missing_docs at crate-root with 'TODO(phase-2-docs)' reasons. Net effect: the warn fires on benten-errors, benten-core, benten-graph, benten-caps, benten-ivm (5/6 — includes the new benten-errors crate from compromise #3). eval + engine still have no enforcement. The reasons are honest about Phase-2 deferral (eval has ~120 pub items, engine ~60) but the discrepancy between 'warn at line 11/40' and 'allow at line 12/41' is the verify-don't-trust pattern in the small. Accept as partially-closed; the four crates where it fires represent real coverage.
pass1_disposition_details.bp-5: .github/workflows/ci.yml:98 sets PROPTEST_CASES=1024 — not the pass-1-recommended 2048, but substantially above the 256 default. Two inline ProptestConfig blocks (benten-caps/tests/proptest_attenuation.rs:57 = 1024 cases, benten-eval/tests/transform_grammar_fuzz.rs:134-137 = 10_000 cases + max_shrink_iters=256) override on a per-test basis. No proptest.toml auto-load file at repo root and no shared test-utils helper. The tail-exploration concern is substantially mitigated at 1024, though the pass-1 recommended 2048 would be one-character change. Accept as partially-closed; Phase-2 bump to 2048 is trivial.
pass1_disposition_details.bp-6: .github/workflows/coverage.yml and .github/workflows/mutants.yml both exist in the workflows directory. Pass-1 finding is closed.
pass1_disposition_details.bp-7: bindings/napi/Cargo.toml:41 still has `napi-build = "2"` as a direct version string in [build-dependencies]. No napi-build entry in [workspace.dependencies]. Unchanged. Still open — trivial drive-by.
pass1_disposition_details.bp-8: package.jsons audited. Root + packages/engine + tools/create-benten-app have `"type": "module"`. bindings/napi correctly omits `"type"` (CJS because napi-rs output). BUT: NONE of the four package.json files declares an `engines` block pinning Node.js. Consumers on Node 18/20 will hit `--experimental-strip-types` failures silently. The scaffolder template (tools/create-benten-app/template/package.json) also ships without engines — generated projects inherit the gap. Still open.
pass1_disposition_details.bp-9: Verified packages/engine/src/* consistently imports from `@benten/engine-native` at the boundary (6 references). No contributor-facing prose warns downstream developers not to import engine-native directly. bindings/napi/package.json has no `exports` block to refuse direct subpath imports. Still open.
pass1_disposition_details.bp-10: Grepped crates/ for `#[diagnostic::on_unimplemented` — zero matches. Grepped for let-else patterns (`let FOO = X else {`) — zero genuine let-else matches (the five hits in benten-eval/src/expr/* are if-else expressions, not let-else). Edition-2024 idioms remain unused. Still open.
pass1_disposition_details.bp-12: package.json scripts still use `node --experimental-strip-types scripts/codegen-errors.ts` and `node --experimental-strip-types scripts/drift-detect.ts`. The scaffolder template for generated projects DOES use `node --import tsx` (correct 2026 idiom), but the workspace-root scripts do not. `tsx` is NOT in the workspace-root devDependencies. Still open.
pass1_disposition_details.bp-13: supply-chain.yml uses EmbarkStudios/cargo-deny-action@v2 (correct per pass-1 suggestion) but still uses `cargo install --locked cargo-audit` in two places. supply-chain-seeded-test.yml uses `cargo install --locked cargo-audit`. ci.yml uses `cargo install --locked cargo-audit` + `cargo install --locked cargo-deny`. mutants.yml uses `cargo install cargo-mutants --locked`. taiki-e/install-action@v2 is used for some tools (nextest) in four workflows. Partial migration. Accept as partially-closed; consolidating to taiki-e/install-action for audit+deny would shave ~5min/run cold-cache.
pass1_disposition_details.bp-14: No insta occurrences outside the pass-1 JSON itself. crates/benten-eval/tests/mermaid_snapshot.rs likely uses inline string comparison (not verified in this pass; bp-14 was a dev-ergonomics suggestion, not a correctness finding). Still open.
pass1_disposition_details.bp-15: SUPPLY-CHAIN.md is NOT present at repo root (verified via ls). The Cargo.toml [patch.crates-io] block at lines 258-260 has in-file prose explaining the core2 + cid patches with pointers to multiformats/rust-multihash#407 and the BentenAI/rust-cid fork. No tracked GitHub issue with a target removal date. Still open.
pass1_disposition_details.bp-16: Cargo.toml has uniform [profile.release] (lto=thin, codegen-units=1, opt-level=3, strip=symbols) and [profile.bench]. No [profile.release.package.benten-core] fat-LTO override. No [profile.test] opt-level=1. Still open — Phase-2 perf-tuning item.

findings.location: crates/benten-errors/src/lib.rs, crates/benten-core/src/lib.rs, crates/benten-graph/src/lib.rs, crates/benten-caps/src/lib.rs, crates/benten-eval/src/lib.rs, crates/benten-engine/src/lib.rs
findings.claim: Zero #[non_exhaustive] attributes on any public enum in the workspace — a 2026 thiserror-2 / API-stability drift.
findings.detail: Grep for #[non_exhaustive] across crates/ returns zero matches. This affects every public error enum (CoreError, GraphError, CapError, EvalError, ViewError, VersionError, EngineError), the ErrorCode enum (the most-likely-to-grow enum in the codebase — compromise #3 literally just extracted it to its own crate for stable-discriminant management), the PrimitiveKind enum (which documents 12 primitive types of which 4 are Phase-2 deferred = guaranteed future variant additions), and InvariantViolation (14 structural invariants declared, 8 of which have Phase-1 enforcement sites — new variants WILL land in Phase 2 as invariants 4/7/8/11/13/14 get implementation). 2026 idiom for public enums in a pre-1.0 library/engine is #[non_exhaustive] on every variant-growing enum so downstream `match` statements written against 0.1 do not fail to compile when 0.2 adds a variant. The verify-don't-trust finding: pass-1 noted thiserror-2 was used correctly but did not verify that #[non_exhaustive] (the other half of the 2026 thiserror-2 idiom) was applied. Every errror variant in docs/ERROR-CATALOG.md is called out as 'stable once shipped' — but without #[non_exhaustive], even adding a new variant for a Phase-2 primitive is a semver-minor-break in Rust-speak.
findings.suggested_fix: Add #[non_exhaustive] to: ErrorCode (benten-errors), CoreError (benten-core), GraphError (benten-graph), CapError (benten-caps), EvalError + InvariantViolation + PrimitiveKind (benten-eval), EngineError (benten-engine). Each change is one line above the `pub enum` declaration. Downstream consumers of these enums already use _ match-arms based on the ErrorCode usage pattern in bindings/napi, so the change is likely non-breaking for internal call sites but catches the semver footgun before Phase 2 adds WAIT/STREAM/SUBSCRIBE/SANDBOX variants.

findings.claim: missing_docs is re-allowed at crate-root immediately after being warned, which defeats the lint on these two crates.
findings.detail: Pattern: `#![warn(missing_docs)]` at line 11, then `#![allow(missing_docs, reason = "TODO(phase-2-docs): ...")]` at lines 12-15 (benten-eval) and `#![warn(missing_docs)]` at line 40 then `#![allow(missing_docs, reason = "...")]` at lines 41-44 (benten-engine). Net effect: the warn annotation reads as 'this crate enforces doc coverage' when actually the lint is muted. The reasons are honest about Phase-2 deferral (~120 + ~60 pub items awaiting post-evaluator re-audit), but the warn/allow pattern is cargo-cult signalling — either remove the warn+allow pair entirely (and just leave a comment noting the Phase-2 plan), or leave only the warn and triage the ~180 missing-doc sites. A reader doing a quick audit of the crate root will misread this as 'missing_docs enforced' when it is not. This is the exact verify-don't-trust pattern on the small scale.
findings.suggested_fix: Option A (honest-no-enforcement): delete the `#![warn(missing_docs)]` line and leave the TODO comment so nobody thinks the lint is active. Option B (partial-enforcement): replace the crate-root allow with per-module `#[allow(missing_docs)]` on specifically the modules still lacking docs, so any new module automatically inherits the warn. Option C (do the work): actually write the ~180 doc comments in the Phase-1 R6 sweep already committed to. Option A is the cheapest; Option B is the most 2026-idiomatic; Option C is the Phase-2 plan of record.

findings.claim: Commit message says 'pruned 95->46' but actual current count is 51 allow entries.
findings.detail: Counted `^[a-z_]+ = "allow"` lines in [workspace.lints.clippy]: 51 matches. Pass-1 and the in-file comment block at Cargo.toml:46-48 say 'down from 95 entries to the genuine Phase-1-scope compromises'. The prune IS real and substantial (95 -> 51 is a ~46% reduction), and every entry now has load-bearing reason prose. The minor drift: the number in the commit message (46) does not match the code (51). Five extra allow lines exist in practice — among them `unwrap_used`, `expect_used`, `cast_possible_wrap` at lines 42-43 and 79, which arguably belong in the kept-list. This is a paper-vs-code drift of the type the agent-definition doc warned about, and the honest fix is update the commit-message reference OR prune five more entries to match the stated 46. Not load-bearing for correctness, but calling it out as the exact verify-don't-trust small-case.
findings.suggested_fix: Either (a) update Cargo.toml lines 46-48 and any downstream commit-message reference to say '51 entries' instead of '46', or (b) prune five more entries to land at exactly 46 (candidates for pruning: cast_possible_wrap — should be deny; default_trait_access — cosmetic; inefficient_to_string — test-only, migrate to per-test allow; unreadable_literal — test-only, migrate to per-test allow; byte_char_slices — cosmetic). Option (a) is one line and closes the drift; option (b) raises the lint bar by five.

findings.claim: The two git-sourced [patch.crates-io] entries (core2, cid) have in-file prose with 'remove once #407 merges' language but no target-date tracking or issue-linked removal trigger. This is the same pattern that let two aspirational compromise claims slip through R5 — prose without a code hook.
findings.detail: The in-file comment at Cargo.toml:242-257 accurately documents the two git-fork patches and points to multiformats/rust-multihash#407 as the upstream PR. The concern: without a tracked issue with a target check-in date OR a Dependabot rule that nudges when the upstream version bumps, the patches could sit for Phase 2/3 without anyone noticing. This is a doc-without-enforcement pattern — exactly the shape of compromise #1 (trait method exists, no executor calls it) and compromise #5 (prose says 'records metrics', no code records). A SUPPLY-CHAIN.md entry with a table {crate, source URL, upstream ticket, target removal date} plus a GitHub issue pinned to 2026-06-01 would give the claim a code-hook. dependabot.yml doesn't (and can't easily) cover patched-to-git entries, so a calendar-driven re-check is the correct control.
findings.suggested_fix: Add SUPPLY-CHAIN.md at repo root with a table: | Patched Crate | Git Source | Upstream Ticket | Target Removal | Last Checked |. Open a GitHub issue titled 'Track: remove core2 + rust-cid patches once upstream merges' and assign a 2026-06-01 first check-in date. Update Cargo.toml's patch block comment to link the issue. This closes the doc-without-enforcement loop for the one supply-chain drift the workspace currently carries.

findings.claim: No tsconfig.json in bindings/napi; the napi-rs auto-generated .d.ts is accepted with no TS-side validation beyond the wrapper's strict compiler.
findings.detail: Listed /Users/benwork/Documents/benten-engine/bindings/napi/tsconfig.json — not present. packages/engine/tsconfig.json exists with strict: true + the full sub-flag set (noUnusedLocals, noUnusedParameters, noImplicitOverride, etc.). Root workspace has no tsconfig.json. Net: the auto-gen'd bindings/napi/index.d.ts is only type-checked transitively via packages/engine (which imports from @benten/engine-native). If a napi attribute in Rust emits an invalid .d.ts shape, the failure surfaces at packages/engine compile time — late. 2026 idiom for an napi-rs v3 binding layer is a minimal bindings/napi/tsconfig.json (target: es2022, strict: true, declaration: false, allowJs: true, checkJs: false) + a npm-script `typecheck` that runs `tsc --noEmit`. CI already invokes vitest on bindings/napi but does not run a dedicated typecheck there. This is pass-1's bp-9 adjacent — the generated .d.ts is the leaky boundary — plus a tsconfig hygiene item not explicitly flagged at pass-1.
findings.suggested_fix: Add bindings/napi/tsconfig.json (minimal: extends the root or packages/engine compilerOptions, overrides declaration=false since nothing is emitted), add a `typecheck` script, wire into ci.yml's js job as `pnpm --filter @benten/engine-native typecheck`. Or more aggressively: use napi-rs's --type-def-header option to assert a stable .d.ts shape and commit a golden index.d.ts (reviewable in PR diffs).

findings.claim: No release-automation tooling (cargo-release / release-plz / manual tag script) is wired. Phase 1 ships 7 workspace crates that all need to go to crates.io in lockstep; the current workflow has no defined path for that.
findings.detail: All seven crates declare `license = "MIT OR Apache-2.0"` and `repository = "..."` so they are crates.io-ready, but the workspace has no [workspace.metadata.release] (cargo-release convention) and no release-plz.toml. 2026 idiom for a multi-crate Rust workspace heading to crates.io is either (a) cargo-release with `shared-version = true` + `publish = true` on public crates + `publish = false` on internal ones (benten-engine is the user-facing crate; the others are implementation detail), or (b) release-plz automation (popular in 2025+ for GitHub-native release flow). Today all seven crates have the same version = '0.1.0' via workspace.package inheritance, but no release command is defined. This becomes load-bearing at Phase 1 ship-day. Pre-emptive setup avoids the 'scramble at release' pattern. Not a Phase-1-R6 blocker.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a release.toml at repo root with the cargo-release config: `shared-version = true`, `consolidate-commits = true`, per-crate `publish` flags (benten-engine = true, others = true for transitive publish; bindings/napi Cargo crate publish = false — it ships via npm). Or adopt release-plz: add `.github/workflows/release-plz.yml` invoking MarcoIeni/release-plz-action@v1. Both are additive and testable via --dry-run before any real publish.

findings.claim: Five `*_used`/lint entries at [workspace.lints.rust] are `allow` at workspace level with no reason prose: unused_must_use, unused_imports, dead_code, unused_variables, non_snake_case.
findings.detail: Cargo.toml lines 23-29: [workspace.lints.rust] has unsafe_op_in_unsafe_fn = "deny" (correct 2026 idiom — one-line below forbid(unsafe_code)). But then five workspace-wide `allow`s with no `reason =` attribute: unused_must_use, unused_imports, dead_code, unused_variables, non_snake_case. Every [workspace.lints.clippy] entry carries a `reason =` prose; the rust-lint entries do not. For workspace-level allows on compiler lints that specifically catch real bugs (unused_must_use on Result, dead_code on never-called fns that probably indicate a refactor miss), the 'reason = ...' prose requirement applies as much as it does for clippy. This is pass-1-bp-1 extended to the rust-lint table. These five lints are also surprisingly broad — `unused_imports = allow` at workspace level silences the ordinary 'you forgot to remove an import' compiler hint.
findings.suggested_fix: Either (a) remove the five allow lines entirely and let rustc fire (any red-phase stubs should carry per-file `#[allow(dead_code, reason = "R3 stub — R5 impl")]`) or (b) add `reason =` prose to each entry explaining the Phase-1 bring-up rationale. Option (a) is more 2026-idiomatic; option (b) is the honest-paper trail. `unused_must_use = allow` in particular is dangerous — it silences the 'you dropped a Result' warning that is load-bearing in benten-graph transaction paths.

findings.claim: Scaffolder template does not include an engines field; generated projects inherit the Node-version-ambiguity bp-8 gap.
findings.detail: tools/create-benten-app/template/package.json (the file the scaffolder copies into the user's new project) has `"type": "module"` correctly but no `"engines": { "node": ">=..." }` block. `create-benten-app` is the 10-minute-DX on-ramp; every project it generates ships without a Node.js LTS pin. The scaffolder's dev-script uses `node --import tsx src/index.ts` which requires a Node version that supports --import (Node 20+). A user on Node 18 (EOL 2025-04) gets a cryptic error instead of an engines-field install-time warning. This is bp-8 applied specifically to the scaffolder template — user-facing surface amplification.
findings.suggested_fix: Add `"engines": { "node": ">=20.0.0" }` to tools/create-benten-app/template/package.json. Also consider: add a `.nvmrc` containing `22` to the template for direnv / nvm users. These two changes propagate to every generated project.

orthogonality_notes:
This pass stayed inside the ecosystem-currency / idiom lens. Noted but not filed as findings (belong to specialist critics):
- GraphError::Redb(String) + GraphError::Decode(String) are retained for test-fixture injection and internal bookkeeping — bp-2 (pass-1) flagged this; the code comment at crates/benten-graph/src/lib.rs:77-83 honestly documents the design. qa-expert should verify the R4-era error-propagation tests still catch the regressions they were designed to catch now that RedbSource is the preferred production path.
- Compromises #1 (cap-refresh at ITERATE boundary) and #5 (per-capability write metrics) were the original motivating examples for 'verify-don't-trust' methodology. They are listed as closed in commits de3f01b and ffa2c5b respectively. This pass did NOT re-verify the fix — that belongs to security-auditor / benten-core-guardian pass-2.
- The pruned clippy allow-list (bp-1 partial) may now allow some real footguns through that the 95-entry version caught indirectly by being permissive about EVERYTHING. A targeted one-time `cargo clippy --workspace --all-targets` did run clean in this verification pass (no output = no warnings), so the current set is self-consistent, but the 'what should have fired but now doesn't' question is correctness-adjacent and belongs to code-reviewer.
notes: Pass-1 verdict was accept-with-minors with 3 majors + 13 minors. Step 5b/5c closed most of the majors and several minors. Pass-2 net: 1 major (bp-17 non_exhaustive, newly found via verify-don't-trust on thiserror-2 idiom), 7 minors (bp-18 through bp-24, mostly small drive-by-ready items). Zero criticals, zero blockers. Verdict: accept-with-minors holds. Recommended Phase-2 queue: bp-17 (non_exhaustive — 1 hour, trivial), bp-18 (missing_docs cleanup on eval/engine — same Phase-2 docs sweep as already tracked), bp-7 (napi-build to workspace.dependencies — 5 minutes), bp-8 + bp-24 (engines fields — 10 minutes across four package.jsons + scaffolder template), bp-23 (rust-lint table reason prose or removal — 15 minutes). The remaining minors (bp-10 diagnostic::on_unimplemented, bp-11 insta, bp-14 insta, bp-15 SUPPLY-CHAIN.md, bp-16 profile tuning, bp-21 napi tsconfig, bp-22 release tooling) are all pre-ship nice-to-haves that do not gate Phase-1 close. The verify-don't-trust methodology worked as designed: bp-17 (non_exhaustive), bp-18 (warn-then-allow), and bp-19 (count drift) are all cases where prior review passes audited the documented claim but did not verify the code enforced it.
